straight_beat.name: Walt Brzezinski
straight_beat.column:
The Tigers beat the Marlins 2-0 yesterday, a game that felt like a microcosm of the season thus far. Skubal pitched seven innings, 12 strikeouts, and allowed just two hits. That’s the kind of performance that should be etched into the ledger. But here’s the thing: the ledger is still filled with more red ink than black.  

The pitching staff has been the story all year. Verlander’s still dominant, sure, but the rotation’s been a mixed bag. Skubal’s outing was a flash of what this team is capable of — a night where the starters are making the other team look like fools. But then there’s the bullpen. They’ve been the albatross hanging around the neck of this team, and it’s not just the occasional meltdown. It’s the consistency, the lack

optimist.name: Patsy Kwiatkowski
optimist.column:
The Tigers beat the Marlins 2-0 yesterday, and let me tell you, that’s the kind of win that makes you slap your thighs and yell, “Detroit’s back, jack!” Not because the record looks great—5-9, three games behind in the Central—but because it’s a sign. A sign that this team, this city, this stubborn, glorious mess of a fanbase is still in the game. Skubal’s slider was the most Detroit thing I’ve seen since the Fox Theatre reopened. That curveball? It’s got the same kind of grit that goes into a paczki—sweet on the outside, sharp on the inside. You can’t help but smile when you see it, even if you’re not sure how it’s working.  

Now, I know what you’re thinking: “Patsy, the record’s still bad.” And you’re right. But here’s the thing—this team’s got a heartbeat. Skubal’s got that slider, and he’s got the poise of a guy who’s been here before. You can’t teach that. You can’t draft it. You can’t even really explain it. You just know when you see it. And when you see it, you think, “This is the start of something.” The bullpen? They’re not perfect, but they’re fighting. That’s the thing about this team. They’re not giving up. Not yet.  

I’ll admit, I’ve been a little obsessed with Skubal lately. Not because I’m some weird fan, but because he’s got that Detroit thing going. You know, the kind of thing that makes you want to throw a parade before the season’s even halfway over. I’ve already picked out my parade outfit—blue and silver, with a little extra glitter for the kids. And let me tell you, the kids are watching. They’re not just watching the games; they’re watching for signs. Signs that this city’s baseball team is finally getting it right.  

The Tigers aren’t perfect. Not by

pessimist.name: Nick Arbogast
pessimist.column:
The Tigers lost again. Not in a way that feels like a loss, really. Just another chapter in the same book, another page in the script they’ve been writing since 2003. Yesterday’s 2-0 defeat to the Marlins wasn’t a loss in the traditional sense—it was a reminder of how the game is played now, how the Tigers are still learning to play it. The Marlins, for all their mediocrity, executed their game plan. The Tigers? They blinked.  

The standings are as damning as ever. 5-9, 3.0 games behind in the AL Central. It’s not just the numbers that sting; it’s the way the numbers cling to the Tigers like a bad cologne. Every spring, they say the kids are coming. Every October, the kids are gone or hurt or traded for more kids. The Tigers are a time loop. You can’t un-ring that bell.  

The front office has spent the last decade trying to figure out what they’re doing wrong. They’ve traded prospects like confetti, drafted players who never pan
